narrative:
Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated an 84% chance that you would not pass based on your academic profile. This high predicted risk of failure triggered the rejection to help the university maintain strong bar passage rates for its students. The model's decision was driven primarily by your class rankings from earlier and later years in school, along with your LSAT performance, which together pointed strongly toward a higher risk of struggling on the bar.

Most influential was your Year 3 class decile of 1, which placed you in the bottom tenth of your peers that year; this stood out as a major red flag because it pushed the prediction heavily toward expecting bar failure, unlike the much higher average decile of about 6 for students who passed. Your Year 1 decile of 2 also contributed in the same direction, signaling early academic challenges that similarly nudged the model toward seeing you as at risk, well below the typical 6 seen among successful bar takers. Even your LSAT score of 30 played a role here, as it fell short of the 37 average for those who passed, further reinforcing the model's concern about potential bar struggles.

In contrast, your undergraduate GPA of 3.20 was close to the 3.24 average for passers, so it didn't sway things much either way, and your lower-middle family income bracket, while less common among bar passers (who are mostly middle to upper-middle), wasn't a top factor. Being full-time aligns with the vast majority of successful students, which is a positive but not enough to offset the key concerns. Compared to typical admitted students—who tend to have stronger deciles around the 6th rank, LSATs near 37, and overall profiles showing consistent high performance—your application appeared riskier in those core academic indicators.

This isn't a judgment on your potential or background, but a data-driven call to protect the program's outcomes. To move forward, focus on boosting your academic deciles through targeted study habits or tutoring, retake the LSAT aiming for 37 or higher, and consider building a stronger narrative in reapplication essays about overcoming early hurdles. With those improvements, you could align much closer to accepted profiles and boost your bar passage odds significantly. Keep pushing—you've got a solid UGPA foundation to build on.
